headlines.title: Power outage impacts thousands of PG&E customers in San Francisco
headlines.description: Thousands of PG&E customers in San Francisco lost power Saturday morning, according to the utility’s outage map. The outage hit the northwest of the city at about 9:52 a.m., including the Presidio, Golden Gate Park and Richmond District. Initially, the utility said about 9,400 customers were affected. According to PG&E, power was restored to all but 2,500 customers by 12:10 p.m. Power was later restored to all customers by 4:02 p.m., the utility announced. PG&E says crews were conducting a routine operational procedure between two substations around 9:45 a.m., when protective device on the system was activated and led to an outage. The San Francisco Department of Energy said traffic signals could be impacted in the area and reminded drivers to treat those intersections as four-way stop signs. PG&E is investigating the cause of the outage.
headlines.source: Bay City News, Christie Smith and Victoria Meza

headlines.title: San Pedro Square in San Jose prepares for large crowds during World Cup final
headlines.description: On Saturday, hundreds of soccer fans gathered in San Pedro Square to watch England beat France 6-4 in a match for third place. Now, fans are preparing for the World Cup final match between Spain and Argentina on Sunday. For weeks, San Pedro Square has been the hot spot for watching world cup soccer, with some people attending nearly all the viewing parties. “We came here plenty of times. It’s always very lively and active. It’s a beautiful thing. I really hope it continues because it’s been so nice,” Ayden Ayala said. An even larger crowd is expected to pour into San Pedro Square on Sunday at noon for the World Cup final: Spain vs. Argentina. After Saturday’s game, many people stayed to hand out near San Pedro Square. They had dinner, bought merch and enjoyed the soccer vibes. NBC Bay Area saw some fans buying Messi shirts as they prepared to return to watch Sunday’s game. Some said they’ve enjoyed the World Cup events so much they hope the city will continue holding other big events that bring the community together. The events have also been a big win for businesses, from hot dog vendors to restaurants and bars.

headlines.title: Bay Area shoppers concerned following cyclosporiasis outbreak
headlines.description: Many shoppers are now paying closer attention to where their food comes from as Taylor Farms removed iceberg lettuce sourced from central Mexico following an FDA investigation linking some of its lettuce to a cyclosporiasis outbreak. Shoppers at the Mountain Farmers Market in Los Gatos said they prefer buying directly from the people who grow their produce. “I love to meet the farmers, and I feel like just knowing where your food comes from is amazing. It just feels really good. It’s beautiful produce,” Los Gatos resident Mary Curtiss said. The concern follows an FDA investigation linking shredded iceberg lettuce served at taco bell restaurants in Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio, and west Virginia to a cyclosporiasis outbreak. Recalls Jul 17 Health Jul 16 Health & Wellness Jul 15 Friday, Taco Bell released a statement after some of its customers reported feeling ill saying it would remove all Taylor Farms affected lettuce from its products. Cyclosporiasis is a parasite that can contaminate fresh produce, causing severe and prolonged diarrhea. The FDA urged customers in those states to avoid shredded iceberg lettuce supplied by Taylor Farms Mexico. Taylor Farms says the FDA traceback points to a single independent farm, representing less than 1% of the nation’s iceberg lettuce supply. But as a precaution, the company says it’s removing all iceberg lettuce from that growing region indefinitely. The FDA says 1,644 people infected with cyclosporiasis who reported eating at taco bell have been reported by the five affected states. Meanwhile, the CDC says it has received reports of 1,645 confirmed domestic cases of cyclosporiasis since May 1, with more than 5,100 additional reports still under review to determine whether they are domestically acquired cases of cyclosporiasis. In California, health officials say there are no known local outbreaks. “The fact that we haven’t seen that happen in California while so much is going in the Midwest, suggests that our food distribution, or the lot of vegetables that was implicated in the Midwest, was not sent to California, because you would expect a lot more people to be sick,” UCSF Infectious Disease Specialist Dr. Peter Chin-Hong said. While California isn’t seeing a spike in cases, Chin-Hong says there are still simple ways to reduce risk. “Use fruits that has a peel like avocados, melons, mangos, oranges, bananas, things you can peel. Because the inside of the fruit generally has very little contact with soil,” he said. For those buying lettuce, Chin-Hong recommends choosing a whole head instead of bagged greens whenever possible and removing the outer leaves before eating. Health officials recommended those who develop severe or prolonged diarrhea to contact their healthcare provider.

headlines.title: Santa Clara County looking to identify man involved in deadly crash in 1986
headlines.description: The Santa Clara County Office of the Medical Examiner is looking to identify and locate the family of a man involved in a deadly crash in Hollister in 1986. Authorities said the crash occurred near Shore Road just west of Lovers Lane in Hollister. The man was in a Chevy Corvair van with two other passengers when the van was struck by a Cadillac El Dorado. The Office of the Medical Examiner says the man was taken to Hazel Hawkins Hospital and then Stanford Medical Center, where he was pronounced dead. They added one of the passengers stated his name was Louis Sanchez, but this could not be confirmed. Authorities said the man was white or Latino, was between 20 and 40 years old, 5 feet, 4 inches tall and 165 pounds. He had dark brown hair, brown eyes and natural teeth in good repair, as well as a brown mustache and no tattoos.

headlines.title: South Bay authorities respond to Trump's voter fraud allegations
headlines.description: The Department of Homeland Security is demanding that California and several other states respond to allegations of widespread voter registration problems involving noncitizens. President Donald Trump leveled the charges during an address from the White House on Thursday. This also comes as Trump is trying to the Save America Act, which would require everyone to provide proof of citizenship in order to register to vote. On Friday, Trump sent the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) after states like California, where they claim close to a quarter of a million non-citizens are on the state’s voter rolls, without saying how they came up with that number. And while a federal judge recently found that DHS wrongly classified a number of actual us citizens who had registered to be non-citizens, Matt Moreles with the Santa Clara County Registrar of Voters said “When this has come up, it’s been really isolated.” DHS says state election officials have to run their voter records through a federal database looking for non-citizens, or face severe penalties, including jail time. “The conversation around are noncitizens registering, are noncitizens voting has been going on for years now, and we still haven’t seen any tangible credible evidence that this is a widespread problem,” Moreles said. According to the Santa Clara County Registrar of Voters, between 90 and 95% of their ballots are now sent by mail. And despite voters across the country, including at times Trump himself, voting successfully by mail, the president also called out that system as fraudulent. “Hopefully there will be no corrupt mail in ballots, which if you watched California and so many other places, mail-in ballots are inherently corrupt,” he said. A charge the county registrar has heard before, but so far with no corruption found. “We have an extremely secure vote by mail process, extremely successful, I think we have a proven track record here in our county,” Merelos said. In response to the accusations, Governor Gavin Newsom spoke out on Friday, going on social media to deny any voter fraud in California and saying Trump’s goal of making people show ID when they vote will help the Republican party. NBC Bay Area also reached out to the Secretary of State’s Office, but have not yet heard back.

headlines.title: Paul Pelosi charged with misdemeanor hit-and-run over collision in Napa County
headlines.description: Paul Pelosi, husband of former House Speaker Nancy Pelosi, was charged Friday with a misdemeanor hit-and-run over a collision with a parked car. Pelosi, 86, was driving his brown convertible July 3 in Yountville, a town in the heart of wine country, when he struck a legally parked car on the side of the road, the Napa County Sheriff’s Office said in a statement at the time. He briefly stopped and then allegedly drove away. The parked car was unoccupied and no injuries were reported. Pelosi did not have any alcohol in his system, according to the statement. State law requires drivers involved in accidents resulting in property damage to stop and provide their vehicle and license information; Pelosi was charged with a misdemeanor violation of that law. In addition, Pelosi was charged with an infraction of making an unlawful turn, Napa County prosecutors said Friday. No attorney for Paul Pelosi was listed in court records. Nancy Pelosi’s press office did not immediately respond to a call seeking comment. Paul Pelosi’s court appearance is scheduled for Aug. 14. Pelosi pleaded guilty in 2022 to misdemeanor charges of driving under the influence in Napa County and was sentenced to five days in jail and three years of probation. He served two days in jail and received good conduct credit for two other days, leaving just one day to serve in a work program at the courthouse. As part of his probation, Pelosi was required to pay thousands in fines and victim restitution, attend a three-month drinking driver class and install an ignition interlock device, which forces drivers to provide a breath sample to prove sobriety before the engine will start. That same year he was attacked and severely beaten with a hammer at the couple’s San Francisco home.

headlines.title: Volunteers in Santa Clara pack 20,000 meals to fight hunger
headlines.description: On Saturday, volunteers in the South Bay were helping their neighbors to make it in the Bay. The nonprofit Islamic Relief USA worked with volunteers at the Muslim Community Association in Santa Clara to put together 20,000 nonperishable meals. Hundreds of volunteers filled boxes with rice, carrots, pinto beans, and seven spices. The organization says it holds similar events throughout the year, bringing people together to fight hunger. The nonprofit provides humanitarian aid and development support to vulnerable communities around the world with programs in more than 40 countries.

headlines.title: More than 300 attend the College Access Summit at San Francisco State
headlines.description: More than 300 students gathered at San Francisco State University Saturday for the College Access Summit. The event includes workshops, campus experiences and college readiness programs to help students prepare for higher education. The summit was hosted by the university and the nonprofit 10,000 Degrees. The partnership focuses on expanding college access for first generation and low-income students. It guarantees admission to San Francisco State for eligible students in the 10,000 Degrees program.

headlines.title: Former deputy charged after alleged sexual assault at San Francisco county jail
headlines.description: A former San Francisco Sheriff’s deputy was charged with sexually assaulting a transgender woman in one of the downtown San Francisco jails, prosecutors said Friday. Damon Jones, 34, was accused of engaging in sexual activity with a confined adult while acting as a peace officer employed in a public detention facility and dissuading a witness from reporting a crime, the San Francisco District Attorney’s Office said. Both are felonies. San Francisco has several county jail facilities. The major housing units are in San Bruno; others are downtown at the Hall of Justice and at 425 7th St., where Jones was assigned, which houses women and a gender-responsive rehabilitation program. Prosecutors said Jones told the transgender woman to go to the gym with him on Sept. 11, 2025. Video surveillance showed Jones walking into a bathroom stall with the victim following him inside. There, she told investigators, he had her perform oral sex on him. Video showed the woman coming out of the bathroom and reaching into her sock, pulling out a plastic bag and spitting into it. The woman told investigators she saved some of the ejaculate in her mouth, which is what she spat into the bag. She gave the bag to her attorney, who took it to a crime lab. DNA testing came back with a match to Jones, prosecutor said. “The victim also told detectives that after the oral copulation, Mr. Jones told her to shut up, that no one would believe her, and not to tell anyone or he ‘would roll her up to F Pod,’ which the victim understood was a threat to transfer her to a unit where she would feel less safe,” according to the District Attorney’s Office. The Sheriff’s Office said Jones was hired as a deputy in June 2024. He was assigned to County Jail 2, where the assault allegedly occurred, in August 2025. Anyone with information regarding the case is asked to call the San Francisco Police Department Tip Line at 415-575-4444 or send a text to TIP411 and begin the message with SFPD.

headlines.title: Crews locate wreckage of boat that sank in San Francisco Bay
headlines.description: Crews on Friday located the wreckage of a boat that sank in San Francisco Bay earlier in the week, leaving two people dead and two others missing, police said. The 49-foot cabin cruiser named Volare was found by the San Francisco Police Department’s Marine Unit in the vicinity of where the boat sank Tuesday afternoon, according to police. The Marine Unit had been utilizing boat-mounted sonar to locate the vessel since it sank, police said. Crews have since turned to using a remotely operated vehicle to assess the wreckage. Police will work with partner agencies to evaluate recovery options and determine if it’s safe and feasible to recover the wreckage, police said. San Francisco Jul 16 San Francisco Jul 16 The boat sank with 20 people aboard after being hit by a wave and capsizing, officials said. The group was on the boat to scatter the ashes of a loved one. One man, Clifford Boisa, died immediately after being retrieved from the chilly water, and three people were listed as missing. On Thursday, police recovered the body of one of the missing people, identified as Tondra Madruga, 58, also known as Tondra Miller. Ralph Boisa said his extended family and some close friends were on the boat Tuesday to celebrate the life of his daughter, who died over a decade ago. Madruga was a friend. The two people who remain missing are Ralph Boisa’s sister, Carol, and Clifford Boisa’s wife, Jackie, he said.
headlines.source: NBC Bay Area staff and The Associated Press

headlines.title: YMCA summer camp counselor accused of sexually assaulting 2nd girl in Morgan Hill
headlines.description: Police said Friday the YMCA summer camp counselor accused of sexually assaulting a girl at a summer day camp in Morgan Hill earlier this month had allegedly done the same thing to a second girl that same day. Morgan Hill police said the report came on July 9, the same day 25-year-old Anthony Joseph Ramos of Gilroy, a YMCA summer camp counselor, was arrested after allegedly sexually assaulting a 10-year-old girl. According to authorities, the second incident occurred while the 10-year-old victim was attending the YMCA summer day camp on a field trip to the Morgan Hill Aquatic Center on the same day as the first victim. Officials said Ramon has been working with YMCA since Aug. 6, 2024. After the first incident, authorities said Ramon was immediately placed on leave. In light of the new report, Ramos was arrested on suspicion of lewd and lascivious acts on a minor on Thursday, according to police. Authorities said he has since posted bail and was released from custody.

headlines.title: Brush fire burns roughly 50 acres near Clayton
headlines.description: A brush fire was burning near Clayton Friday afternoon. The blaze, which broke out near Morgan Territory and Marsh Creek roads, has scorched roughly 50 acres and was burning with a moderate rate of spread, according to Cal Fire. Authorities added the fire is 10% contained. The fire began shortly before 3 p.m., and Contra Costa Fire officials said it moved quickly, with the terrain making it difficult for crews to access. “First units that arrived found a rapidly moving fire moving uphill in somewhat difficult conditions,” Contra Costa County Fire Chief Aaron McAlister said. It prompted an evacuation warning to Morgan Territory State Park and Mount Diablo State Park. Authorities warned residents in the area they should prepare to evacuate. Morgan Territory Road was temporarily closed before being reopened Friday evening. For residents like Juan Rios, who says this area has a history of brush fires, any evacuation warning is enough to get his attention. “We get pretty concerned because we’re surrounded with pine trees, our houses can be burned down in a heartbeat,” he said. Firefighters expect to be out here overnight, working to build containment lines and keep the fire from spreading. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.
